1: “This is the account of the family line of Esau (that is, Edom).” The toledot formula — the same structure that frames every major genealogy in Genesis — is given to Esau’s line. But unlike the toledot of Jacob (Genesis 37:2), which opens into the great Joseph narrative, Esau’s toledot is genealogy and closure. The chapter exists to account for Esau’s line and close it out, so that what follows in Genesis tracks only the covenant line. Election is real exclusion as well as real inclusion: God’s choice of Jacob is not arbitrary but purposive, and the closing of Esau’s narrative in Genesis 36 is the narrative form of that exclusion. Romans 9:13 — “Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated” — is the theological summary of the movement from Genesis 36 to Genesis 37.
2: Esau’s wives from Canaan — contrary to the explicit guidance that defined the covenant line (Genesis 24:3; Genesis 28:1). The first note about Esau’s descendants marks the non-covenant character of his line: he marries those from whom the covenant people were to remain distinct. The covenant identity is not primarily ethnic but covenant-relational — defined by faithfulness to the God of the promise. Esau’s Canaanite wives are the first signal that his line will follow a different path. The contrast with Jacob, who sought wives from within the covenant family (Genesis 28-29), marks the two lines apart from the beginning.
3: Basemath, daughter of Ishmael — Esau marries within the other rejected line (Ishmael was excluded from the primary covenant inheritance, though blessed). The rejected lines intermingle: Esau’s Ishmaelite wife produces a branch of his genealogy. Both Ishmael and Esau received divine blessings — Ishmael became a great nation (Genesis 21:18), Esau received the prosperity described in this chapter — but neither received the covenant promise of the seed through whom all nations would be blessed. The distinction between temporal blessing and covenant election runs throughout Genesis and finds its NT culmination in the one who is both the fulfillment of the covenant and the blessing of all nations (Galatians 3:16, 29).
4: Eliphaz and Reuel — the two major lines of Esau’s descendants through Adah and Basemath. Eliphaz is the name of the first and wisest of Job’s three friends (Job 2:11) — a Temanite (v15), which matches this genealogy. The wisdom of Edom (Jeremiah 49:7 — “Is there no longer wisdom in Teman?”) is well attested; the Edomites had a wisdom tradition that ran parallel to Israel’s. The general revelation available to all people (Romans 1:20) means that wisdom is not confined to the covenant people — but the covenant people receive the special revelation that alone leads to salvation (Romans 10:14-17). Christ is “the wisdom of God” (1 Corinthians 1:24) that both fulfills and surpasses the wisdom of Edom.
5: The sons born to Esau in Canaan before the move to Seir — Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. The family established in the land of promise, then leaving. The irony of Esau settling in Canaan and then departing, while Jacob returns to Canaan and settles: the land of promise is for the covenant heir, not the rejected line. The motif of the wrong person in the right place is corrected by the movement of history: Esau leaves; Jacob remains. The covenant people’s attachment to the promised land is not merely ethnic or geographic but theological — it points toward the new creation and the city that is to come (Hebrews 11:10; Revelation 21:2).
6: Esau took all his household, livestock, and possessions accumulated in Canaan and moved to a land away from his brother Jacob. The separation of the two brothers — the elect and the non-elect — through the natural mechanism of insufficient grazing land (v7). Divine providence operates through mundane economic pressures to accomplish the separation that the covenant structure requires. The God who chose Jacob over Esau does not need supernatural intervention to accomplish the separation; the ordinary pressures of pastoral life suffice. The separation serves the covenant: Jacob must inhabit Canaan; Esau must be elsewhere. Providence is often quiet.
7: “Their possessions had become too great for them to remain together; the land where they were staying could not support them.” The same dynamic separated Abraham and Lot (Genesis 13:6): the covenant land requires its people to separate from the non-covenant people as blessing accumulates. Lot chose Sodom; Esau chooses Seir. In both cases, the separation that looks economically motivated is actually covenantally necessary: the covenant heir must have the land. The “too great to remain together” language echoes the later Exodus concern (Exodus 1:9 — “the Israelites have become far too numerous for us”). Blessing multiplies until separation becomes necessary.
8: Esau settled in the hill country of Seir — he is Edom, he is there. The geographical establishment of Edom in Seir closes the first movement of the chapter. Seir is the mountain country southeast of Canaan that becomes Edom’s permanent territory. The Edomites and Israelites will be in conflict throughout the historical books: Saul fights Edom (1 Samuel 14:47), David subdues Edom (2 Samuel 8:14), Edom revolts against Jehoshaphat (2 Kings 8:20). The hostility between brothers that begins in the womb (Genesis 25:22-23) takes geographical form here. The NT resolution: “there is neither Jew nor Gentile… you are all one in Christ Jesus” (Galatians 3:28) — the covenant-versus-non-covenant division is overcome in Christ.
9: The second toledot heading within the chapter — “the account of Esau the father of the Edomites.” The doubled introduction signals that Genesis 36 is a comprehensive genealogical record of Esau’s legacy, not a narrative episode. The thoroughness of the record shows that God keeps account of all nations, not only Israel. Acts 17:26 — “from one man he made all the nations, that they should inhabit the whole earth; and he marked out their appointed times in history and the boundaries of their lands.” The Edomite genealogy is the form of that divine ordering of the nations. God’s sovereignty extends to the table of nations, not only to the covenant community.
10: Eliphaz son of Adah and Reuel son of Basemath — the two main branches of Esau’s line through his wives. The genealogical record of the rejected brother is preserved in Scripture alongside the covenant genealogy of Jacob. This preservation is itself a form of grace: Esau is not erased but remembered. The God who chose Jacob did not annihilate Esau — he blessed him with many descendants and territory, in fulfillment of the blessing Isaac gave (Genesis 27:39-40). The non-elect are not abandoned by God; they receive the blessings of common grace. Christ’s atonement is sufficient for all, though effective for the elect (1 John 2:2 — “the atoning sacrifice for our sins, and not only for ours but also for the sins of the whole world”).
11: The sons of Eliphaz: Teman, Omar, Zepho, Gatam, and Kenaz. Teman is the most prominent — a city in Edom famous for its wisdom (Jeremiah 49:7; Amos 1:12). Eliphaz the Temanite of Job 2:11 carries the wisdom of this region. The “wisdom of the east” that the magi represent (Matthew 2:1) is part of the general revelation that God dispersed among the nations. The magi’s wisdom ultimately led them to Christ — the wisdom that Edom had without the covenant was insufficient without the covenant people’s scripture and the star of Bethlehem. All human wisdom finds its goal in Christ (Colossians 2:3 — “in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge”).
12: Timna was a concubine of Eliphaz, who bore him Amalek. Amalek — the people who became Israel’s perennial enemy, attacking from behind (Deuteronomy 25:17-18), whom God commanded to be blotted out (1 Sam 15). The Amalekite lineage that begins here becomes the line of Haman the Agagite (Esther 3:1 — Agag was the Amalekite king of 1 Sam 15) who seeks to destroy all the Jews in Persia. The spiritual pattern of the enemy who attacks the weak and pursues the covenant community across history is the pattern that culminates in the dragon who seeks to devour the woman’s child (Revelation 12:4). Every Amalek points toward the final enemy that Christ defeats (Revelation 20:10).
13: The sons of Reuel: Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah. Reuel is also the name of Moses’ father-in-law (Exodus 2:18) — an Edomite/Midianite connection. The interweaving of the covenant history with Edomite family names is a reminder that God’s saving purposes are not sealed in a hermetic ethnic enclosure. Moses’ connections to Midian/Edom are part of his formation; the wisdom and hospitality of Jethro (another name for Reuel) are gifts that God uses within his providential plan. The covenant community is always entangled with the nations around it — and Christ is the one in whom all entanglements are redeemed (Ephesians 1:10 — “to bring unity to all things in heaven and on earth under Christ”).
14: The sons of Oholibamah: Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. Korah is also the name of the Levitical rebel (Numbers 16:1) who led the revolt against Moses and Aaron — different person, same name. The recurrence of names across covenant and non-covenant lines is a reminder that the human family is one family. The rebellion pattern is not exclusive to the non-covenant nations; the covenant people are equally capable of the Korah-style revolt against divinely appointed leadership. The NT warning about following Korah’s example (Jude 11) is addressed to the church. Sin and rebellion are human problems, not merely Edomite ones. Christ alone is the one who does not rebel but fulfills (Matthew 5:17).
15: The chiefs among Esau’s descendants through Eliphaz. The listing of chiefs (ʾallûfîm) — the organizational structure of Edomite tribal society. The political organization of the nations is God’s provision for human order after the Fall: the structures of authority that restrain chaos are part of common grace. Romans 13:1 — “the governing authorities that exist have been established by God.” The Edomite chiefs represent the human authority structures that God permits and orders — not covenant mediators, but providential organizers of human community. Christ is the one in whom all legitimate authority is grounded (Colossians 1:16 — “all things were created through him and for him”) and to whom all authority will be submitted (1 Corinthians 15:25).
16: Chief Korah, Chief Gatam, and Chief Amalek among the Edomite chiefs. The listing of Amalek as a chief in Edom formalizes what v12 introduced: Amalek is a recognized political entity within Edomite society. The national enemies of Israel are not random — they have genealogical, political, and historical identities. The enemy that attacks the covenant community is a real, organized entity. The NT equivalent is not a tribal confederation but spiritual powers (Ephesians 6:12 — “our struggle is not against flesh and blood, but against the rulers, against the authorities, against the powers of this dark world”). Christ has disarmed these powers (Colossians 2:15) and his final victory will end the enmity that began in the womb (Genesis 25:22-23).
17: Chiefs descended from Reuel — Nahath, Zerah, Shammah, and Mizzah — all Edomite chiefs. The comprehensive listing shows that God’s ordering of the nations is thorough — not only the covenant people are organized under his providence, but also the nations that surround them. The sheer completeness of the Edomite record points to the divine care that extends even to those outside the covenant. Acts 14:17 — “he has not left himself without testimony: he has shown kindness by giving you rain from heaven and crops in their seasons.” The Edomite chiefs receive the common grace of political organization, agricultural provision, and territorial settlement — all from the God of Israel, whether they acknowledge him or not.
18: Chiefs descended from Oholibamah — Jeush, Jalam, and Korah. The three chiefs through Esau’s third wife complete the enumeration of Edomite leadership. The third wife’s descendants receiving the same dignified enumeration as the others reflects the biblical pattern of comprehensive accounting: no branch of the human family is beneath divine notice. The parable of the lost sheep (Luke 15:4-7) — leaving ninety-nine to find the one — operates on the assumption that every individual counts. The Edomite genealogy is the covenant God’s comprehensive accounting of the human family, even the line that did not receive the covenant promise.
19: “These were the sons of Esau (that is, Edom), and these were their chiefs.” The summary that closes the enumeration of Esau’s immediate descendants and chiefs. The Esau/Edom identification — repeated through the chapter — is the narrative equivalent of the divine word “Esau I hated” (Malachi 1:3). Edom is the name that history gives to the rejected line; the name encodes the rejection while preserving the person. Jacob becomes Israel — the covenant name replaces the birth name. Esau becomes Edom — the ethnic name replaces the personal name. The rejected line endures under its own identity, outside the covenant. The elect line is transformed by the covenant encounter. Christ is the one through whom Jacob’s transformation becomes available to Edomites (Galatians 3:28).
20: The Horites — the original inhabitants of the Seir hill country before Esau’s line displaced them. Deuteronomy 2:12 notes that “the Horites used to live in Seir, but the descendants of Esau drove them out.” Esau’s line displaces the Horites just as the Israelites will displace the Canaanites — the same pattern at a smaller scale, without covenant justification. The Horite genealogy that follows (vv20-30) is included because Esau’s family intermarried with the Horites (Oholibamah was a Horite, vv2, 14). The entanglement of the elected Edomites with the Horite indigenous population reflects the complex social realities through which divine providence works. No human community exists in pure isolation.
21: The Horite chiefs: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. The indigenous people of Seir receive their own genealogical accounting — they are not merely the unnamed background against which Esau’s arrival is staged. The biblical pattern of including the genealogies of displaced peoples (the Horites here, the table of nations in Genesis 10) reflects the covenant God’s comprehensive interest in all human communities. Christ died for Horites as well as Israelites — the “every tribe, language, people and nation” of Revelation 5:9 includes peoples long absorbed and forgotten by history but not by God.
22: Lotan’s sons: Hori and Homam. Timna was Lotan’s sister — the same Timna who became Eliphaz’s concubine (v12) and bore Amalek. The genealogical link between the Horites and Edomites through Timna shows the social integration of the two groups. The covenant community’s own genealogy includes similar entanglements: Tamar (Genesis 38), Ruth (a Moabite), Rahab (a Canaanite), and Bathsheba (wife of a Hittite) all appear in Matthew’s genealogy of Christ (Matthew 1:2-16). The genealogy of the Messiah includes the outsider precisely because Christ’s mission is to bring the outsider in. The Horite connections in Esau’s genealogy are the dark mirror of the Gentile connections in Jesus’ genealogy.
23: The sons of Shobal: Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam. The Horite genealogy continues with the thoroughness that the text gives to all genealogical records — a thoroughness that reflects the biblical conviction that human identity is located in family, ancestry, and community. The Horites are not nameless masses; they are individuals with specific family lines. This specificity is the form of biblical anthropology: people are known in their particularity, not as abstract humanity. Christ himself entered human particularity — born of a specific woman, in a specific town, of a specific lineage (Matthew 1:1). The incarnation is the ultimate affirmation of human particularity as the location of divine encounter.
24: Zibeon’s sons: Aiah and Anah. “This is the Anah who discovered the hot springs in the desert while he was grazing the donkeys of his father Zibeon.” The parenthetical note — a single act of discovery preserved in the genealogy. The springs (yemim — possibly “mules” or “hot springs” — the word is uncertain) are the one thing Anah is remembered for. The genealogy of the nations preserves small acts alongside great ones. Every human life has moments that define its place in the larger story. Christ entered a world where Anah’s springs had been flowing for centuries — a world with deep, specific history in every direction, not a blank stage for salvation history to play out upon.
25: Anah’s children: Dishon and Oholibamah daughter of Anah. Oholibamah — one of Esau’s wives (v2) — is identified as a granddaughter of Zibeon through Anah. The family connections that make Esau’s three wives representatives of different strands within the regional population. The social complexity of ancient Near Eastern family structures is preserved honestly in the genealogical record. The biblical text does not idealize the families of the patriarchs or their connections; it records what happened. The same honesty marks the NT: James and John asked for the chief seats (Matthew 20:20-21); Peter denied Christ three times (Luke 22:61). The covenant community’s history is recorded with pastoral realism, not hagiography.
26: The sons of Dishon: Hemdan, Eshban, Ithran, and Keran. The enumeration of Horite families continues with the same care as the Edomite enumeration. The inclusion of the Horites within the Edomite chapter reflects the social reality of Esau’s settlement in Seir: he did not arrive in an empty land but in a land with existing communities. The displacement of the Horites by Esau’s descendants was a process of gradual absorption and assimilation as much as conquest. The organic growth of communities through intermarriage, economic integration, and gradual displacement is the normal mechanism of ancient social change — and divine providence works within it, not against it.
27: The sons of Ezer: Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan. The Horite genealogy approaches its end — three more names in the record of the displaced indigenous people of Seir. The care with which these names are preserved reflects the biblical conviction that human identity matters to God regardless of covenant status. The God who counts the hairs of every head (Luke 12:7) is the God who preserves Bilhan, Zaavan, and Akan in the text of Scripture. The gospel that Christ brings is not only for those with historical claim to the covenant but for every person whose name is recorded somewhere — including names recorded only in a genealogy that most readers skip.
28: The sons of Dishan: Uz and Aran. Uz — the same name as the land of Uz where Job lived (Job 1:1). The connections between the patriarchal narratives and the wisdom literature run through genealogical names: Eliphaz (Job’s friend) is Esau’s son (v11); Uz (Job’s homeland) appears as a Horite name here. The wisdom tradition that Job represents has roots in the populations of the Edomite region — wisdom as a form of general revelation available to those outside the covenant community. The wisdom of Uz and the suffering of Job are part of the same regional tradition that God uses to teach his people about suffering, righteousness, and divine sovereignty. Christ answers Job’s questions not by explanation but by resurrection (1 Corinthians 15:20).
29: The Horite chiefs: Lotan, Shobal, Zibeon, Anah, Dishon, Ezer, and Dishan. The summary of the Horite chiefs parallels the summary of the Edomite chiefs (vv15-19). The parallel structure of the two lists — both organized as chiefs of a people, both presented with the same genealogical formula — gives the Horites a dignity equal to the Edomites in the text’s presentation. The God who orders all nations orders both the covenant-adjacent (Edomites) and the fully outside (Horites) with the same providential care. The gospel is for all nations precisely because all nations are known to the God who orders them: “all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me. Therefore go and make disciples of all nations” (Matthew 28:18-19).
30: “These were the Horite chiefs, according to their divisions, in the land of Seir.” The Horite section closes with the same formula as the Edomite section: enumerated, organized, summarized. The land that the Horites occupied has now been defined as Seir — which is also Edom. The overlap of names for the same territory (Seir = Edom = the hill country of the rejected line) reinforces that Esau’s settlement was a displacement, not a discovery. The covenant people will also be a displacing presence in Canaan — the same dynamic, with covenant authorization. The territorial dimensions of God’s ordering of humanity are resolved eschatologically in the new creation, where “the nations will walk by [the Lamb’s] light” (Revelation 21:24).
31: “These were the kings who reigned in Edom before any Israelite king reigned.” The kings of Edom precede the kings of Israel — Edom is politically mature before Israel has a monarchy. The anticipatory reference to the Israelite king — a king not yet on the horizon in the narrative (Jacob’s sons are not kings) — points ahead to the Davidic monarchy. The kings of Edom who reign before Israel had a king are the foil against which the Davidic kingship will be measured. And the Davidic kingship is itself the foil against which the messianic king is measured (Isaiah 9:6-7; Luke 1:32-33). The Edomite kings list, read in this trajectory, points toward the one King whose kingdom will have no end.
32: Bela son of Beor became king of Edom. Bela son of Beor — the identical name-pattern as Balaam son of Beor (Numbers 22:5), the prophet hired to curse Israel. Whether this is coincidence or a connection, the association of the name “Beor” with both the first Edomite king and the hired prophet who was compelled to bless Israel instead of curse (Numbers 23:8) is suggestive. The God who turns the curser’s mouth into blessing (Numbers 23:20) is the God who will ultimately turn Edom’s enmity into reconciliation. The messianic prophecy of Numbers 24:17 — “a star will come out of Jacob; a scepter will rise out of Israel. It will crush the foreheads of Moab, the skulls of all the people of Sheth” — comes from the mouth of a man named like this first Edomite king.
33: Jobab son of Zerah from Bozrah succeeded Bela as king. Bozrah — the prominent Edomite city that appears in prophetic literature as the emblem of Edom’s pride and the object of divine judgment. Isaiah 63:1 — “Who is this coming from Edom, from Bozrah, with his garments stained crimson? Who is this, robed in splendor, striding forward in the greatness of his strength?” — the divine warrior coming from Bozrah in judgment. In Amos 1:12 and Isaiah 34:6, Bozrah is the site of divine judgment on Edom. The city that provides the second Edomite king also provides the setting for one of the most vivid images of divine redemptive judgment in the prophets — an image that Revelation 19:13 applies to Christ (“he is dressed in a robe dipped in blood”).
34: Husham from the land of the Temanites succeeded Jobab as king. Teman — the wisdom center of Edom (v11, v15), the homeland of Eliphaz the Temanite of Job, the city whose wisdom Jeremiah 49:7 eulogizes. A king from Teman brings the wisdom tradition of Edom to the throne. The covenant people are not the only source of human wisdom — but their wisdom, grounded in the fear of the LORD (Proverbs 1:7), is distinctive. The wisdom of Teman was real; the wisdom that could not save Edom was not wrong but incomplete. Christ as the one in whom “are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3) is the completion of all partial wisdoms — including Teman’s.
35: Hadad son of Bedad, who defeated Midian in Moab, succeeded Husham as king. The first Edomite king with a military achievement recorded: the defeat of Midian in Moab. The Edomite kingdom is strong enough to project power into neighboring territories. The irony that Hadad’s military victory is the one historical note in the Edomite king list: the covenant people’s victories are recorded in the context of divine deliverance; the Edomite victory is recorded as the king’s own achievement. The difference between the theology of military success in Deuteronomy (“it is not by your own sword and bow” — Joshua 24:12) and the Edomite attribution of military success to the king is the difference between covenant and non-covenant historiography.
36: Samlah from Masrekah succeeded Hadad as king. The succession of Edomite kings without dynastic continuity — each new king comes from a different city and apparently a different family — contrasts with the Davidic covenant promise of dynastic permanence (2 Samuel 7:12-16: “your house and your kingdom will endure forever before me”). The Edomite kingship is elective or competitive, not dynastic. The messianic promise is dynastic and eternal — a king not like the Edomite kings who come and go, but one whose throne endures forever. The contrast between the succession list of Edom and the genealogy of the Davidic line points toward the one king who is also the eternal Son (Psalms 2:7; Hebrews 1:5).
37: Shaul from Rehoboth on the river succeeded Samlah as king. Rehoboth — a place-name meaning “broad places” or “room.” The same name appears in Isaac’s well-digging narrative (Genesis 26:22): “Now the LORD has given us room, and we will flourish in the land.” The covenant promise of space/room for the covenant people contrasts with the king of Rehoboth “on the river” (possibly the Euphrates) — an Edomite king from a boundary territory. The “room” that Isaac celebrated as divine gift is here the name of a king’s city. The providential provision of room for the covenant people has an analogue in the territorial prosperity of Edom, even without the covenant. Common grace provides room for all; the covenant provides the meaning of that room.
38: Baal-Hanan son of Akbor succeeded Shaul as king. “Baal-Hanan” — a name that includes the name of the Canaanite fertility god, Baal. The Edomite king whose name invokes Baal is a window into the religious world of the surrounding nations — the same Baal worship that will be the greatest internal temptation for Israel (1 Kgs 18-19; Elijah vs. the prophets of Baal). The covenant people’s vulnerability to Baal worship was always related to their proximity to the nations whose kings were named after Baal. Christ as the Lord who conquers Baal — not by Elijah’s fire from heaven but by the definitive victory of the resurrection — is the answer to the long Baal problem in Israel’s history.
39: Hadad succeeded Baal-Hanan and his wife was Mehetabel, daughter of Matred, daughter of Me-zahab. The last king in the pre-Israelite list — and the one whose wife’s lineage is noted, the only such notation in the list. Me-zahab means “waters of gold” — a name suggestive of luxury or wealth. The final Edomite king’s wife comes from a named lineage, giving her a dignity that the other queens are not given. The listing of the woman’s lineage alongside the king’s reflects the biblical pattern of honoring women within genealogical records. Ruth, Tamar, Rahab, and Bathsheba are named in Christ’s genealogy (Matthew 1:3-6); the women of Edom’s royal line receive their record here.
40: The chiefs of Esau by name and clan — a second list of chiefs (the first was in vv15-19) organized differently, apparently by territorial region rather than family descent. Chiefs Timna, Alvah, Jetheth — the first three of eleven chiefs in vv40-43. The double listing — first by family (vv15-19), then by territory (vv40-43) — shows the two organizing principles of tribal society: kinship and land. The covenant community also organized by both tribe (kinship) and allotted territory (the land of Canaan divided among the twelve tribes in Josh 13-19). The organizational patterns of all human communities reflect the basic realities of family and land — both of which find their eschatological fulfillment in the family of God and the new earth (Revelation 21:1-4).
41: Chiefs Oholibamah, Elah, Pinon — three more Edomite territorial chiefs. The name Oholibamah appears both as one of Esau’s wives (v2) and as a territorial chief here — likely named after the woman who founded the family. The naming of territories after founding mothers is a form of memorial that the biblical text preserves without embarrassment. The women whose names become place-names in Edom are not the mothers of the covenant promise, but they are the mothers of real human communities. The God who knows the number of hairs on every head (Luke 12:7) knows Oholibamah’s name and the community she founded. Her memorial is in Scripture.
42: Chiefs Kenaz, Teman, Mibzar — including Teman and Kenaz, which were also names of Eliphaz’s sons (v11). The overlap of personal names and territorial/clan names shows the fluid relationship between genealogy and geography in ancient Israelite record-keeping: a region often takes its name from a founding ancestor, or a person’s name becomes identified with the region they inhabited. Kenaz — a name also carried by Caleb’s ancestor among the Israelites (Numbers 32:12: “Caleb son of Jephunneh the Kenizzite”) — appears in both Edomite and Israelite lineages. The overlap of names across covenant and non-covenant communities is a reminder that the covenant people were drawn from the same human pool as those outside the covenant.
43: “Magdiel and Iram. These were the chiefs of Edom, according to their settlements in the land they occupied. This was Esau the father of the Edomites.” The final verse of Genesis 36 closes the Edomite record with its territorial summary and the identifying phrase “Esau the father of the Edomites.” From Genesis 36 onward, the narrative leaves Edom and follows Jacob’s family — beginning immediately in 37:1 with “Jacob lived in the land where his father had stayed, the land of Canaan.” The transition from Esau to Jacob — from the rejected line to the covenant line — marks the movement toward the Joseph narrative and through it toward the Exodus, Sinai, and ultimately Christ. Ch36 is the door that closes on one story so the main story can continue.
